final_payload.description: To satisfy the layer preconditions, cookieValue(name) must be invoked with a non-null cookie name that exists in the parsed cookie list returned by cookie(name). The request instance must also have a non-null headers list containing at least one entry whose key is exactly "Cookie"; the first such Cookie header’s value must be the malicious cookie header string. Using duplicate cookies with the same name ("SID") and conflicting attributes (notably differing $Path assignments) can cause the downstream CookieDecoder/parser to mis-associate attributes across cookie instances. This inconsistency can lead to an integer overflow when computing Max-Age for one of the cookies (e.g., parsing an extremely large Max-Age), causing getMaxAge() to return Integer.MIN_VALUE. By calling cookieValue("SID"), the code retrieves the cookie via cookie("SID") and returns cookie.getValue(), ensuring the vulnerable parsing path is exercised when cookies are decoded from the Cookie header.

HttpRequestWrapper.cookieValue(name) is a pure delegate: it returns this.request.cookieValue(name). To satisfy the layer goal and propagate the sink requirement to the downstream cookie decoder, this.request must be a non-null org.webbitserver.stub.StubHttpRequest whose headers list contains a Cookie header with a crafted cookie header string. The crafted Cookie header includes multiple cookie definitions using the same cookie name but with conflicting attribute-like tokens (e.g., $Path) so that the downstream InboundCookieParser/CookieDecoder mis-associates attributes across duplicates. This parsing inconsistency drives the decoder into an integer overflow path when interpreting Max-Age-like data, ultimately producing Integer.MIN_VALUE from getMaxAge(). The method parameter 'name' must be non-null and equal to the duplicated cookie name so that request.cookieValue(name) selects one of the parsed HttpCookie objects with getName() == name, ensuring the maliciously-parsed cookie is actually looked up and returned/used.

To satisfy this layer’s preconditions and preserve the sink-level constraint, we supply an HttpUriRequest whose getURI() returns a URI whose user-info contains an extra '@'. This creates an ambiguous authority section (user-info vs host delimiter) that can later be misparsed by Apache HttpClient URIUtils.extractHost() / HttpHost parsing logic, potentially yielding a null/incorrect host. In this method, that crafted URI is propagated to request.getURI() (used in the warning log path) and is also the URI used by httpClient.execute(request, context), thereby ensuring downstream target-determination logic sees the malicious authority string. The context is set to null (allowed by constraints) and charset is assumed to be a valid non-null encoding (UTF-8 per template).

Cannot satisfy the layer template’s preconditions with the provided method body. In cn.qs.utils.weixin.auth.WeixinJSAPISignUtils.main(String[]), the args parameter is never read (args[0] is not accessed) and is not passed to WeixinInterfaceUtils.getJsapiTicket(); instead, getJsapiTicket() is invoked with no arguments. Therefore, no concrete value for args[0] (including a FastJSON '@type' payload) can influence execution in this layer, and the stated precondition 'args[0] must be a valid string that when passed to WeixinInterfaceUtils.getJsapiTicket()...' is inconsistent with the actual call site. To proceed toward the FastJSON auto-type JSON-string sink-level constraint, we need the source/signature of WeixinInterfaceUtils.getJsapiTicket() and/or the downstream deserialization sink that consumes a JSON string, because this main() method does not accept or forward any attacker-controlled JSON input.

The method InstallTemplate.run() itself takes no parameters; it just calls execute() and, on IOException, prints "Unable to install template: " + folder.get(0). To satisfy the layer goal preconditions for the downstream vulnerable sink (FilenameUtils.normalize), the instance field 'folder' must be a List<String> with exactly one element, and that element must be a Windows UNC path that includes directory traversal segments (..\) which can cause FilenameUtils.normalize to return null (a known edge case for UNC + traversal). A concrete payload is a UNC path starting with \\HOST\share followed by repeated '..\\' segments; this can bypass normalization/validation logic and yield a null normalization result, enabling subsequent path traversal issues if null is mishandled.

The wrapper method org.webbitserver.wrapper.HttpRequestWrapper.cookies() is a simple delegate that returns this.request.cookies(). To satisfy the layer goal and propagate the malicious cookie header into the underlying cookie parser/decoder, the only necessary setup is to ensure this.request is a org.webbitserver.stub.StubHttpRequest whose headers list contains a "Cookie" entry. That Cookie header value is crafted to include multiple cookie definitions with the same name ("sid") and conflicting attribute assignments (e.g., $Path and Max-Age). This duplication/attribute confusion is intended to trigger inconsistent parsing in the downstream CookieDecoder such that getMaxAge() overflows and yields Integer.MIN_VALUE. When cookies() is invoked, the StubHttpRequest implementation will read the Cookie header and pass it to the decoder, exercising the vulnerable parsing logic.
